summary:
The Sweet Track is a prehistoric wooden walkway or causeway preserved in the peat bogs of the Somerset Levels in southwestern England, dated by dendrochronology (tree-ring dating) to the winter of 3807–3806 BCE — making it one of the oldest known engineered roads in the world. Constructed by early Neolithic communities associated with the [[event:1588|Windmill Hill culture]], the Sweet Track ran for approximately 1,800 meters across a marshy wetland, providing a raised walkway above the boggy ground between two areas of higher, drier land.

The track was constructed by driving wooden poles into the peat and laying split planks and round wood across them, creating a surface elevated above the wetland. The construction used a variety of tree species including ash, oak, hazel, elm, and alder — suggesting systematic woodland management and exploitation of diverse timber resources. Objects found along and beneath the track include a jadeitite axe (probably imported from the Alps via continental Europe), flint tools, pottery, and what may be a bow — providing a snapshot of the material culture of its Neolithic builders.

The preservation of the Sweet Track in the anaerobic peat of the Somerset Levels is extraordinary: wooden objects, fiber, and even human footprints have survived for nearly six thousand years. The track demonstrates a high level of engineering skill and planning in Neolithic Britain and provides direct evidence of the organized community effort that could be mobilized by early farming societies. It is a scheduled ancient monument and UNESCO World Heritage property.
